pi_items.source: Multiple sources: Sigma-Aldrich, ChemicalBook, EPA IRIS, NIST, AccuStandard
NIST Chemistry WebBook: Tfus = 256.0 ± 0.6 K = -17°C ± 0.6 K; Average of 8 measurements
Logical inference: Melting point (-17°C) < 20°C, therefore liquid at 20°C; Multiple sources confirm it is a colorless liquid at room temperature
Multiple sources: Sigma-Aldrich, ChemicalBook, EPA IRIS, AccuStandard
ChemicalBook: -25°C to -22°C (literature range); Web search results confirm same range
Logical inference: Melting point range (-25 to -22°C) < 20°C, therefore liquid at 20°C; Multiple sources describe as colorless liquid
Multiple sources: Sigma-Aldrich, ChemicalBook, EPA IRIS, NIST, AccuStandard
ChemicalBook: 52-54°C; Web search results: 53.5°C, 53.09°C, 52.7°C; Task value of 53°C is within range and matches central values
Logical inference: Melting point (53°C) > 20°C, therefore solid at 20°C; Multiple sources describe as white or colorless crystalline solid at room temperature
Standard ambient temperature per IUPAC and industrial standards
Task execution scripts confirm this path; Setup script creates directory at /home/ga/Documents

privileged_info_summary: All metadata claims are verified against authoritative chemical sources. 1,2-Dichlorobenzene (CAS 95-50-1) has a melting point of -17°C (confirmed by NIST WebBook as -17°C ± 0.6 K, average of 8 measurements), making it liquid at 20°C. 1,3-Dichlorobenzene (CAS 541-73-1) has a melting point range of -25 to -22°C with a task-specified value of -24.8°C within this range, also liquid at 20°C. 1,4-Dichlorobenzene (CAS 106-46-7) has a melting point of 53°C (within verified range of 52-54°C), making it solid at 20°C. All three CAS numbers are confirmed correct. Physical state designations at 20°C are thermodynamically correct based on melting points. The data is from real, authoritative chemical databases (NIST, EPA, ChemicalBook, Sigma-Aldrich) and is not synthetic.
